---
title: "Building a Bulk Email Dispatcher in Shiny"
description: "A reproducible walkthrough for sending hundreds of personalised HTML emails from a Shiny app — with rate limiting, test mode, a live progress bar, and Outlook COM automation."
author: "Andrew Fuller"
date: "2026-05-20"
categories: [R, Shiny, Email, Tutorial]
image: "thumbnail.png"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

Suppose you need to send a few hundred personalised emails every month — each one containing
a colour-coded summary table tailored to the recipient. Maybe it's monthly performance
reports going to regional office managers, or data submission reminders going to partner
organisations.

You *could* sit in Outlook and copy-paste. Or you could build a Shiny app that handles the
whole workflow: previewing the campaign, test-sending to yourself, and dispatching the real
thing with a live progress bar and built-in rate limiting.

This post is a fully reproducible walkthrough of how to build one. Every code block below
uses synthetic data you can generate yourself — no external database or credentials required
to follow along.

***

## Setup

Install the packages we'll use:

```r
install.packages(c(
  "shiny", "dplyr", "gt", "rmarkdown", "blastula",
  "waiter", "shinyalert", "shinyjs"
))

# RDCOMClient is needed for Outlook COM automation (Windows only).
# Install from GitHub if not already available:
# install.packages("RDCOMClient", repos = "https://nexus.cs.ucdavis.edu/repository/r-proxy/")
```

::: {.callout-note}
`RDCOMClient` only works on Windows with Outlook installed. If you're on macOS/Linux or
want a portable solution, skip to [What I'd Do Differently] at the end for SMTP alternatives.
:::

***

## Step 1: Create Synthetic Data

First, let's generate some fake data to work with — a set of regional offices, each with
monthly episode counts and contact details.

```{r}
#| code-fold: false

library(dplyr)

set.seed(42)

# 20 regional offices across two programme types
offices <- tibble(
  office_id   = 1:20,
  office_name = paste("Region", LETTERS[1:20], "Office"),
  programme   = rep(c("Inpatient", "Outpatient"), each = 10),
  is_active   = sample(c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE), 20, replace = TRUE)
)

# Monthly submission counts for the last 12 months
months_cols <- format(seq.Date(as.Date("2025-06-01"), by = "month", length.out = 12), "%b-%y")

submissions <- offices |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(data = list(setNames(
    as.list(sample(c(0, 0, rpois(10, lambda = 15)), 12, replace = TRUE)),
    months_cols
  ))) |>
  ungroup() |>
  tidyr::unnest_wider(data)

# Contact list — some offices have multiple contacts
contacts <- bind_rows(
  tibble(
    office_id = rep(1:20, each = 2),
    name      = paste("Contact", 1:40),
    email     = paste0("user", 1:40, "@example.com")
  )
) |>
  # drop a few to simulate missing contacts
  slice_sample(prop = 0.85)
```

Here's a peek at our data:

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
submissions |> select(office_id, office_name, programme, all_of(months_cols[1:4]))
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
contacts |> head(8)
```

***

## Step 2: Building the Email Plan

Before sending anything, we build a **plan** — one row per office, with the recipient
addresses and the data slice that will go into that office's email.

```{r}
#| code-fold: false

prepare_email_plan <- function(
  submissions,
  contacts,
  filter_active    = TRUE,
  filter_programme = NULL
) {

  data <- submissions
  if (filter_active)        data <- data |> filter(is_active)
  if (!is.null(filter_programme)) data <- data |> filter(programme == filter_programme)

  # One row per office, with a pre-sliced data frame and contact list

  plan <- data |>
    group_by(office_id, office_name, programme) |>
    summarise(across(everything(), first), .groups = "drop") |>
    rowwise() |>
    mutate(
      office_contacts = list(contacts |> filter(office_id == .env$office_id)),
      to_emails       = paste(
        unique(office_contacts$email[!is.na(office_contacts$email)]),
        collapse = ";"
      ),
      contact_count   = n_distinct(office_contacts$email[!is.na(office_contacts$email)])
    ) |>
    ungroup()

  list(
    plan = plan,
    stats = list(
      total_offices   = nrow(plan),
      total_contacts  = sum(plan$contact_count),
      by_programme    = plan |>
        group_by(programme) |>
        summarise(offices = n(), contacts = sum(contact_count), .groups = "drop")
    )
  )
}

result <- prepare_email_plan(submissions, contacts)
result$stats
```

Key design choices:

1. **Pre-sliced data** — each row carries only that office's data, so the email template
   never touches the full dataset
2. **Contact counts drive rate limiting** — one "office email" going to 3 people counts as
   3 sends against the rate limit (more on this below)
3. **Filtering is parameterised** — the Shiny UI can toggle active-only, by programme, etc.

***
## Step 3: The Email Template

Each email is rendered from a parameterised R Markdown document that produces an HTML email
with a colour-coded summary table.

Create a file called `email_template.Rmd`:

**Front matter** (`email_template.Rmd`):

```yaml
# email_template.Rmd
output: blastula::blastula_email
params:
  office_name: "Example Office"
  programme:   "Inpatient"
  report_data: NULL
  deadline:    "2026-08-14"
```

**Template body** (the single R chunk inside the `.Rmd`):

```r
library(gt)
library(dplyr)

report_data <- params$report_data

# Identify the month columns (everything that looks like "Mon-YY")
month_cols <- grep("^[A-Z][a-z]{2}-\\d{2}$", names(report_data), value = TRUE)

tbl <- report_data |>
  select(office_name, programme, all_of(month_cols)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = paste(params$programme, "Submission Summary"),
    subtitle = paste("Deadline:", params$deadline)
  ) |>
  cols_label(office_name = "Office", programme = "Programme")

# Colour-code: red = 0, amber ≤ 5, green ≥ 15
for (col in month_cols) {
  tbl <- tbl |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffc7ce")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] == 0)
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffeb9c")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] > 0 & .data[[col]] <= 5)
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#c6efce")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] >= 15)
    )
}

tbl
```

The colour thresholds are simple here (0 / ≤5 / ≥15) but you can adjust them to whatever
makes sense for your domain. The important thing is that the template is *parameterised* —
the calling code passes in the data slice, and the template just renders it.

::: {.callout-tip}
`blastula::blastula_email` produces self-contained HTML that renders well in most email
clients. The `{gt}` table's CSS is inlined automatically.
:::

***

## Step 4: Sending with Rate Limiting

Microsoft 365 enforces a sending rate of roughly 30 messages per minute for most tenants.
To stay safely within that, we target **250 emails per hour** (≈ 1 every 14.4 seconds).

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false

send_all_emails <- function(
  plan,
  test_mode            = FALSE,
  test_email           = "your-test-inbox@example.com",
  target_rate_per_hour = 250,
  session              = NULL
) {

  sleep_per_email <- (60 * 60) / target_rate_per_hour   # 14.4 seconds
  total   <- nrow(plan)
  sent    <- 0
  failed  <- 0

  for (i in seq_len(total)) {
    row <- plan[i, ]

    tryCatch({
      # 1. Render the email template for this office
      tmp_rmd <- tempfile(fileext = ".Rmd")
      file.copy("email_template.Rmd", tmp_rmd)

      rmarkdown::render(
        tmp_rmd,
        params = list(
          office_name = row$office_name,
          programme   = row$programme,
          report_data = row,           # the pre-sliced single-row data frame
          deadline    = "2026-08-14"
        ),
        quiet = TRUE
      )

      email_html <- paste(readLines(sub("\\.Rmd$", ".html", tmp_rmd)), collapse = "\n")

      # 2. Determine recipients
      to <- if (test_mode) test_email else row$to_emails
      subject <- paste0(
        if (test_mode) "[TEST] ",
        row$programme, " submission summary — ", row$office_name
      )

      # 3. Send via Outlook COM automation
      outlook  <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application")
      mail     <- outlook$CreateItem(0)          # 0 = MailItem
      mail[["To"]]       <- to
      mail[["Subject"]]  <- subject
      mail[["HTMLBody"]]  <- email_html
      mail$Send()

      sent <- sent + 1

      # 4. Update progress (if inside a Shiny session)
      if (!is.null(session)) {
        session$sendCustomMessage("updateProgress", list(
          current = i,
          total   = total,
          percent = round((i / total) * 100, 1),
          message = paste("Sent to", row$office_name)
        ))
      }

      # 5. Rate-limit sleep — scaled by number of contacts
      Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * max(row$contact_count, 1))

    }, error = function(e) {
      failed <<- failed + 1
      message(sprintf("[%d/%d] FAILED: %s — %s", i, total, row$office_name, e$message))
    })
  }

  list(sent = sent, failed = failed, total = total)
}
```

A few things to unpack:

### Rate limiting math

The `Sys.sleep()` at the end of each iteration scales by `contact_count` — because an email
going to 3 people counts as 3 sends against Microsoft's rate limit:

```r
sleep_per_email <- (60 * 60) / 250   # = 14.4 seconds per "send"

# Office with 3 contacts → sleep 14.4 × 3 = 43.2 seconds
Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * row$contact_count)
```

### Outlook COM automation

`RDCOMClient` drives Outlook as a COM object. The minimum required:

```r
outlook <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application")
mail    <- outlook$CreateItem(0)        # 0 = MailItem
mail[["To"]]       <- "a@example.com;b@example.com"  # semicolon-separated
mail[["Subject"]]  <- "Your monthly report"
mail[["HTMLBody"]]  <- "<h1>Hello</h1>"
mail$Send()
```

Requirements:

- Outlook must be installed on the machine running the app
- The R process must run as a user with an Outlook profile configured
- If using `SentOnBehalfOfName`, the user needs delegate permissions for that address

***

## Step 5: Test Mode

Before sending to real recipients, you want a **test mode** that redirects everything to
your own inbox. The pattern is simple — thread a single `test_mode` boolean through the
entire stack:
```{r}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false

# When test_mode = TRUE:
to <- if (test_mode) test_email else row$to_emails
subject <- paste0(if (test_mode) "[TEST] ", subject)
```

In a Shiny context, gate the send behind a confirmation dialog:

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false

shinyalert::shinyalert(
  title = "Send Test Campaign?",
  text = htmltools::tagList(
    htmltools::div(
      class = "alert alert-info",
      htmltools::h4("TEST MODE — SAFE TO SEND"),
      htmltools::p(
        htmltools::strong("All emails will be sent to: "),
        htmltools::code("your-test-inbox@example.com")
      )
    ),
    htmltools::p("\u2713 Subject lines prefixed with [TEST]"),
    htmltools::p("\u2713 No database updates will be made")
  ),
  html = TRUE,
  type = "info",
  showCancelButton = TRUE,
  callbackR = function(confirmed) {
    if (confirmed) {
      send_all_emails(plan, test_mode = TRUE, test_email = "your-test-inbox@example.com")
    }
  }
)
```

When `test_mode = TRUE`:

1. **Recipients** are replaced with your sandbox address
2. **Subject lines** get a `[TEST]` prefix
3. **Database updates are skipped** — no flags are flipped, so the real campaign can still run

This lets you send 5 test emails to yourself, verify them in Outlook, and then dispatch the
real campaign knowing nothing has been touched.

***

## Step 6: Live Progress Feedback

The trickiest UX piece: showing progress while R is blocked inside a `for` loop.

The approach uses the `{waiter}` package for a full-screen overlay, with a custom HTML
progress bar that gets updated via Shiny's `sendCustomMessage()`:

### R side — send updates from the loop

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false

# Inside the send loop (already shown in Step 4):
session$sendCustomMessage("updateProgress", list(
  current = i,
  total   = total,
  percent = round((i / total) * 100, 1),
  message = paste("Sent to", row$office_name)
))
```

### JavaScript side — receive and render

Add this to your app's `www/` directory (or inline via `tags$script()`):

```javascript
Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("updateProgress", function(data) {
  var text = document.getElementById("progress-text");
  var bar  = document.getElementById("progress-bar");
  var msg  = document.getElementById("progress-message");

  if (text) text.textContent = data.current + " / " + data.total + " emails sent";
  if (bar)  bar.style.width  = data.percent + "%";
  if (msg)  msg.textContent  = data.message;
});
```

### Waiter overlay setup

```{r}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false

library(waiter)

create_progress_waiter <- function() {
  Waiter$new(
    html = htmltools::tagList(
      htmltools::div(
        class = "waiter-content-center",
        htmltools::h4(id = "progress-text", "Preparing to send emails..."),
        htmltools::div(
          class = "progress",
          style = "width: 80%; margin: 20px auto;",
          htmltools::div(
            id    = "progress-bar",
            class = "progress-bar progress-bar-striped active",
            role  = "progressbar",
            style = "width: 0%; transition: width 0.4s ease;"
          )
        ),
        htmltools::p(id = "progress-message", "Initialising...")
      )
    ),
    color   = "rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.92)",
    fadeout = TRUE
  )
}
```

The result: a full-screen overlay that smoothly updates with the office name and a
percentage bar as each email is dispatched.

***

## Estimated Wait Time

Before confirming a production send, show the user how long they'll be waiting:

```{r}
#| code-fold: false

# Using our earlier plan
total_contacts    <- result$stats$total_contacts
seconds_per_email <- (60 * 60) / 250             # 14.4 seconds
wait_seconds      <- total_contacts * seconds_per_email
wait_minutes      <- wait_seconds / 60

cat(sprintf(
  "Sending to %d contacts across %d offices.\nEstimated time: %s",
  total_contacts,
  result$stats$total_offices,
  if (wait_minutes >= 60) paste0(round(wait_minutes / 60, 1), " hours")
  else paste0(round(wait_minutes, 1), " minutes")
))
```

***

## Putting It All Together

Here's a minimal but complete Shiny app that ties these pieces into a three-step workflow:
**Build Plan → Test Send → Production Send**.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Full Shiny app (click to expand)"
#| eval: false

library(shiny)
library(shinyjs)
library(shinyalert)
library(waiter)
library(dplyr)

ui <- fluidPage(
  useShinyjs(),
  useShinyalert(),
  useWaiter(),

  tags$head(tags$script(HTML('
    Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("updateProgress", function(data) {
      var text = document.getElementById("progress-text");
      var bar  = document.getElementById("progress-bar");
      var msg  = document.getElementById("progress-message");
      if (text) text.textContent = data.current + " / " + data.total + " emails sent";
      if (bar)  bar.style.width  = data.percent + "%";
      if (msg)  msg.textContent  = data.message;
    });
  '))),

  titlePanel("Bulk Email Dispatcher"),

  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      checkboxInput("active_only", "Active offices only", TRUE),
      selectInput("programme", "Programme",
                  choices = c("All", "Inpatient", "Outpatient"),
                  selected = "All"),
      textInput("test_email", "Test inbox", value = "your-test-inbox@example.com"),
      hr(),
      actionButton("btn_plan", "1. Build Plan", class = "btn-primary", width = "100%"),
      br(), br(),
      actionButton("btn_test", "2. Test Send", class = "btn-warning", width = "100%"),
      br(), br(),
      actionButton("btn_send", "3. Send Production", class = "btn-danger", width = "100%")
    ),
    mainPanel(
      verbatimTextOutput("plan_summary"),
      tableOutput("plan_table")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  rv <- reactiveValues(plan = NULL, stats = NULL)

  observeEvent(input$btn_plan, {
    prog_filter <- if (input$programme == "All") NULL else input$programme
    result <- prepare_email_plan(submissions, contacts,
                                  filter_active = input$active_only,
                                  filter_programme = prog_filter)
    rv$plan  <- result$plan
    rv$stats <- result$stats
  })

  output$plan_summary <- renderPrint({
    req(rv$stats)
    cat(sprintf("Offices: %d | Contacts: %d | Est. time: %.1f min\n",
                rv$stats$total_offices, rv$stats$total_contacts,
                rv$stats$total_contacts * 14.4 / 60))
    print(rv$stats$by_programme)
  })

  output$plan_table <- renderTable({
    req(rv$plan)
    rv$plan |> select(office_name, programme, to_emails, contact_count)
  })

  observeEvent(input$btn_test, {
    req(rv$plan)
    shinyalert("Test Send?",
               paste("All emails will go to:", input$test_email),
               type = "info", showCancelButton = TRUE,
               callbackR = function(ok) {
                 if (ok) send_all_emails(rv$plan, test_mode = TRUE,
                                          test_email = input$test_email,
                                          session = session)
               })
  })

  observeEvent(input$btn_send, {
    req(rv$plan)
    shinyalert("Send Production?",
               paste(rv$stats$total_contacts, "emails will be sent to REAL recipients."),
               type = "warning", showCancelButton = TRUE,
               callbackR = function(ok) {
                 if (ok) send_all_emails(rv$plan, test_mode = FALSE, session = session)
               })
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

***

## What I'd Do Differently

1. **Use SMTP instead of COM** — `RDCOMClient` requires Outlook on the server, which is
   awkward in containers or on non-Windows machines. [`Microsoft365R`](https://github.com/Azure/Microsoft365R)
   provides a Graph API client that works anywhere, or you can use a proper SMTP relay with
   `{blastula}` or `{emayili}`.

2. **Run the send loop asynchronously** — `send_all_emails()` blocks the R process. Wrapping
   it in `future::future()` with `promises` would keep the Shiny session responsive, though
   pushing progress updates from a background process takes extra plumbing
   (e.g., writing progress to a file and polling with `reactivePoll()`).

3. **Add resume-from-failure** — if the loop errors at email 150 of 200, you currently have
   to re-run the whole campaign (relying on an "exclude already sent" filter). An explicit
   checkpoint — writing sent IDs to a log file and skipping them on restart — would be
   more robust.

***

## Packages Used

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `RDCOMClient` | Outlook COM automation (Windows only) |
| `rmarkdown` | Render the HTML email template per office |
| `blastula` | `blastula_email` output format for email-friendly HTML |
| `gt` | Colour-coded summary table inside the email |
| `waiter` | Full-screen loading overlay during send |
| `shinyalert` | Confirmation dialogs (test vs production) |
| `shinyjs` | Disable/enable buttons during the send loop |
| `dplyr` | Data wrangling and plan construction |

***

All the code above is self-contained — generate the synthetic data, create the template file,
and you've got a working email dispatcher. Swap in your real data source, adjust the `{gt}`
thresholds, and you're production-ready.

If you're building something similar, feel free to reach out via
[GitHub](https://github.com/andrew-fuller).
